hook: A church tower ruin growing straight out of the Roman city wall
facts: The garden preserves standing Roman and medieval wall, part of the Cripplegate fort's north side
Church first recorded c.1108-25, closed and demolished by Act of Parliament around 1600
Laid out as a public garden in 1837; flint-and-rubble central tower survives with arches on three sides
Salters' Hall, opened 1976, frames the wall to the north
w: Layered like an archaeological cake: Roman defensive wall at the base, medieval brickwork above, and a flint church tower sprouting from the top, arches gaping on three sides. Closed by Act of Parliament around 1600 and turned to garden in 1837. Two thousand years of wall, holding up a ruin.

hook: A Wren church shipped stone by stone to Missouri, leaving only its footprint
facts: Gutted in the 1940 Blitz, leaving walls standing
About 7,000 stones numbered and shipped to Fulton, Missouri in 1965-66 and rebuilt at Westminster College
The London footprint survives as a garden with Wren's foundations and interior column bases visible
Sits beside Guildhall at the junction of Aldermanbury and Love Lane
w: Wren built it, the Blitz gutted it, and in 1965 someone numbered 7,000 stones, shipped them to Fulton Missouri, and rebuilt the whole church there as a Churchill memorial. What stayed behind: the bare footprint, foundations and column stumps, planted as a quiet garden by Guildhall. A church that emigrated.

hook: A 1688 carving of a naked boy on a bread basket, claiming the City's highest ground
facts: Relief in Panyer Alley, off Newgate Street near St Paul's station
Inscription reads 'When ye have sought the citty round yet still this is the highest ground August 27 1688'
Commemorates the Panyer Boy Inn lost in the Great Fire of 1666
Alley named for the panniers, or bread baskets, once made there
legend: The 'highest ground' boast is disputed: Cornhill is actually higher, so it may mean highest only within the old walls
w: A small stone boy sits naked on a bread basket in a nothing alley by St Paul's, dated 1688 and bragging that you'll find no higher ground in all the City. You will, actually, Cornhill beats it, but the pride outlived the inn it once marked. Carved cockney swagger, in flint, and demonstrably wrong.

hook: A hidden paved courtyard behind a Wren church, set with salvaged Roman pavement
facts: Church rebuilt under Wren 1695-1712; 'Foster' is an Anglicisation of the Flemish saint Vaast
The tucked-away courtyard occupies former graveyard ground and was once the site of the Fountain pub
Contains Roman pavement recovered from the demolished St Matthew Friday Street
Reached by a passage between the church and its parish hall
w: Slip down the passage beside this Wren church, named for an obscure Flemish saint whose name the English mangled into 'Foster,' and you reach a paved court with a wall fountain, set on old graveyard ground. Underfoot, salvaged Roman pavement from a long-demolished church nearby. A courtyard built from leftovers.

hook: England's heraldry HQ, still granting coats of arms from a 1670s brick courtyard
facts: Founded by royal charter of Richard III in 1484; re-established by Mary I in 1555
Present building dates 1670-77, after the earlier Derby Place burned in the Great Fire
Heralds still research and grant arms here under the Earl Marshal, the Duke of Norfolk
Queen Victoria Street later sliced through the south end of the original courtyard
w: Behind iron gates on Queen Victoria Street, heralds with titles like Rouge Dragon and Bluemantle still research pedigrees and grant coats of arms, exactly as Richard III chartered them to in 1484. The Great Fire took the first building; this brick courtyard replaced it by 1677. Medieval bureaucracy, fully operational.

hook: A church named for a maypole, holding the tomb of London's first historian
facts: Named for a giant maypole, the 'shaft', once raised yearly beside it, taller than the tower
Maypole abandoned after the 1517 Evil May Day riots, destroyed as a 'pagan idol' in 1547
Survived both the Great Fire and the Blitz; rebuilt around 1532
Holds the monument and grave of John Stow, author of the 1598 Survey of London
w: Its odd name comes from a maypole, the 'shaft', once raised here each spring taller than the tower, until a 1547 mob sawed it up as a pagan idol. Inside lies John Stow, who walked and catalogued Tudor London street by street. Every few years the Lord Mayor renews the quill pen in his stone hand.

hook: Milton's burial church, marooned among Barbican towers beside the Roman wall
facts: First church here built in 1090; 'without' meaning outside the Cripplegate
John Milton buried here in 1674; Oliver Cromwell married here in 1620
Bombed in the Blitz and restored within the 1960s-70s Barbican development
Roman fort bastions and a lake marking the old city ditch sit just outside
w: Milton lies buried here; Cromwell married here in 1620. The medieval church burned in the Blitz, then found itself stranded inside the concrete of the Barbican, a stone survivor ringed by brutalist towers. Outside, a Roman bastion and an ornamental lake trace the line of the old city ditch. Old bones, new walls.

hook: A lone Gothic Wren tower stranded on a traffic island, now a private home
facts: All that survives of St Alban Wood Street after Blitz bombing cleared the rest
Rare Gothic-style design by Wren, with pinnacles at each corner of the 92ft tower
Now stands on a traffic island and is used as a private residence
Faces the former City of London Police headquarters at Love Lane
w: Traffic streams around it on both sides: a 92ft Gothic tower, pinnacles and all, marooned alone in the middle of Wood Street. The Blitz took the rest of Wren's church; only this fairy-tale spike remained. Someone now lives inside it. A church tower turned roundabout, turned house.

hook: Gateway church to medieval London Bridge, with stones from the old crossing in its yard
facts: Stood at the head of Old London Bridge; everyone crossing into the City passed its door
Rebuilt by Wren after the Great Fire, completed 1687; his most expensive parish church
Two stones from Old London Bridge, found in 1921, stand in the churchyard
Holds a large detailed model of the medieval bridge
w: For centuries every soul crossing Old London Bridge into the City walked past this door; the bridge roadway ran right by its tower clock. Wren's costliest parish church, it now keeps two weathered stones from the vanished bridge in its yard, and a model of the houses-on-a-bridge that Eliot called 'inexplicable splendour'.

hook: Dick Whittington's burial church, with the famous cat in a stained-glass window
facts: Richard 'Dick' Whittington, four-time mayor, funded its rebuilding and was buried here around 1423
Rebuilt by Wren after the Great Fire; 'Royal' from La Reole, a wine-trading district name
A stained-glass window near the door shows Whittington with a cat
Whittington's college of priests on College Hill gave the street its name
legend: The pantomime cat is folklore; the real Whittington was wealthy-born and probably never owned one
w: Dick Whittington, four times Lord Mayor, poured money into rebuilding this church and was buried here around 1423. The pantomime says he climbed from rags with a magic cat; the truth says he was born rich and probably catless. Either way, a stained-glass moggy sits by the door, honouring a legend the facts never quite killed.

hook: A tiny medieval church flattened by an IRA bomb, rebuilt as a peace centre
facts: One of few medieval City churches to survive the Great Fire and Blitz; first recorded 1250
Stood seven metres from the IRA truck bomb of 24 April 1993 and collapsed
Painstakingly rebuilt and reopened a decade later as a Centre for Reconciliation and Peace
Among the smallest churches in the City
w: This pocket-sized medieval church dodged the Great Fire and the Blitz, then in 1993 an IRA truck bomb seven metres away brought it down. Rather than clear the rubble, they rebuilt it, stone by stone, and reopened it as a centre for reconciliation. Wreckage turned, deliberately, into a workshop for peace.

hook: A 1671 clock where two giants club the bells, watched by London's oldest outdoor statue
facts: Clock of 1671, the first public clock in London with a minute hand
Two giants strike the hours and quarters and turn their heads
Courtyard holds King Lud and his sons, rescued from the demolished Ludgate
Its statue of Elizabeth I (1586) is thought to be London's oldest outdoor statue
legend: The two club-wielding giants are sometimes said to represent Gog and Magog
w: On the hour, two carved giants swing their clubs at the bells and turn their heads, working a 1671 clock, the first in London with a minute hand. Tucked in the courtyard stand King Lud and his sons, evicted from the old Ludgate, and an Elizabeth I of 1586 reckoned the oldest outdoor statue in town. Fleet Street's mechanical theatre.

hook: Wren's dome rehearsal for St Paul's, centred on a Henry Moore stone altar
facts: Wren's domed prototype for St Paul's, the dome resting on twelve Corinthian columns
A circular travertine altar by Henry Moore was installed during the 1978 restoration
Moore's marble came from the same quarry Michelangelo used
The Samaritans were founded here by rector Chad Varah in 1953
w: Wren practised here: this airy dome on twelve columns rehearsed the one he'd later raise over St Paul's. At its heart squats a round altar Henry Moore carved from travertine quarried where Michelangelo cut his marble, divisive then, beloved now. And in the crypt in 1953, a rector founded the Samaritans by a single telephone.

hook: A gilded grasshopper hanging over Lombard Street since the days before house numbers
facts: Family device of Thomas Gresham, founder of the Royal Exchange in 1565
Hangs outside number 68, marking the site of Gresham's house
Survives from the era before Lombard Street was numbered in 1770, when signs named the buildings
Passed down through occupiers to the bank that became Martins Bank
legend: Folklore says an abandoned infant Gresham ancestor was found in a field by the chirp of a grasshopper
w: Before street numbers, buildings wore signs, and Tudor financier Thomas Gresham hung his family grasshopper over his Lombard Street house. It still dangles, gilded, above number 68. Legend claims a foundling Gresham ancestor was discovered in a field by a grasshopper's chirp. A bug that founded a banking dynasty, still chirping in gold.

hook: A 1799 street pump marking a medieval well and a vanished house of correction
facts: Erected 1799, funded by the Bank of England, the East India Company and local fire offices
Inscription records a well here in 1282 and a House of Correction built by Mayor Henry Wallis
Carries the marks of four fire insurers: Sun, Phoenix, London and Royal Exchange
Designed by Nathaniel Wright, architect of St Botolph Aldersgate
w: Easy to walk past: a Georgian iron pump on Cornhill, paid for in 1799 by the Bank of England, the East India Company and the fire offices, whose insurance marks it still wears like sponsor logos. Its inscription remembers a well here in 1282 and a medieval house of correction. Centuries of City thirst, frozen mid-handle.
